segments.title: Queen Elizabeth Maximus design setup
segments.summary: The stream opens by turning a Royal Navy Queen Elizabeth counterfactual into a testable heavy-gun battleship design.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke frames the exercise as a way to test a more aggressive Queen Elizabeth-style battleship than the historical Royal Navy chose. He notes that the model uses game constraints, including 16-inch guns in place of the 16.5-inch weapons he is discussing, and that the design is heavier and less restrained than a strictly historical build. The useful point is the design logic: a plausible Royal Navy counterfactual still has to wrestle with displacement, layout, secondary armament, and the limits of what the simulation can represent.

evidence.note: The stream introduces the British Empire vessel, Queen Elizabeth Super Maximus, and the use of UAD values.
The design is compared with Monarch/Tegetthoff-style opponents and caveated around 16-inch versus 16.5-inch guns.

segments.title: Austro-Hungarian response to a heavier Queen Elizabeth
segments.summary: An Austro-Hungarian opponent design shows how gun caliber, triple turrets, armor, and oil firing shape a plausible capital-ship reply.
segments.body: This watch point uses the Austro-Hungarian Navy as the immediate design foil for a more heavily armed Royal Navy battleship. Dr. Clarke reasons through triple turrets, a modest speed increase, a 15-inch main battery, armor thickness, and the likelihood of oil firing. The segment is useful because it treats a counterfactual arms response as an engineering and industrial problem, not simply as a bigger-gun contest.

evidence.note: The Austrian design is framed around triples, speed, and a response to a Royal Navy ship with 16.5-inch guns.
The transcript discusses 15-inch guns, armor, turret protection, Italian concerns, and oil firing.

segments.title: Elswick gun orders and hidden calibers
segments.summary: A research aside explains why a suspected 18-inch gun program may be hard to spot in archive order books.
segments.body: The stream briefly turns from the battle setup to the archival problem behind the Queen Elizabeth Maximus idea. Dr. Clarke suggests that a sensitive 18-inch gun development path might not appear under an obvious label and could instead be hidden under special-order wording. For learners, this is a useful reminder that naval technical history often depends on understanding archive naming conventions as much as reading a simple list of calibers.

evidence.note: The transcript discusses Elswick order books and the possibility of an 18-inch program being listed under a disguised label.
The aside compares 13.5-inch, 14-inch special, and foreign-order cover possibilities.

segments.title: King George V alternative without Chatfield's choice
segments.summary: A King George V redesign walks through displacement, armor, machinery, range, secondary guns, and off-the-shelf compromises.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke builds a King George V alternative by starting from displacement and speed, then working through protection, propulsion, range, and secondary armament. He keeps comparing the simulation options with historical Royal Navy equipment, including the absence of a 5.25-inch option and the temptation to use six-inch secondaries. The value here is seeing how a seemingly simple alternative battleship quickly becomes a chain of linked compromises.

evidence.note: The KGV design begins with displacement, speed, oil firing, machinery, underwater protection, and armor figures.
The transcript covers missing 5.25-inch guns, six-inch alternatives, fire-control equipment, and range assumptions.
The resulting design is described as an off-the-shelf alternative better than the historical KGV, with caveats about secondary guns.

segments.title: Goliath heavy-gun layout tradeoffs
segments.summary: An extreme heavy-gun design shows why turret placement, engine efficiency, secondaries, and weight savings matter even in a deliberately exaggerated battleship.
segments.body: The Goliath design is deliberately excessive, but the design discussion still exposes real naval-architecture constraints. Dr. Clarke experiments with forward firepower, turret spacing, engine efficiency, bulkheads, torpedoes, and secondary batteries, repeatedly noting where the game permits arrangements that would be awkward or unrealistic in steel. This time note is useful for watching how an apparently overwhelming main battery still has to be balanced against protection, machinery, and usable arcs of fire.

evidence.note: The design is built around heavy forward firepower, armor, and engine-efficiency concerns.
The transcript discusses unrealistic layouts, efficient gun arrangements, aft fire, bulkhead cuts, and weight savings.
The final setup adds torpedoes, faces classification problems, and moves toward a custom battle test.

segments.title: Range control and secondary-battery lessons
segments.summary: The battle tests show how speed, range control, main-battery volume, and secondary-gun range can decide a capital-ship duel.
segments.body: The final battle tests turn the design work into a lesson about engagement envelopes. Dr. Clarke first highlights how speed and a fifteen-gun main battery let Ajax fight at a range that suits her armor and firepower while keeping enemy secondaries irrelevant. A later closer fight reverses the lesson: once seven-inch secondaries are brought into range, the same design starts taking damaging hits, showing why setup, scouting, screens, and range control matter as much as headline gun caliber.

evidence.note: Ajax is described as a 28-knot heavy-gun design, and the transcript connects the number of guns to accuracy and volume of fire.
The transcript explicitly explains speed, firepower, armor envelopes, and staying just outside secondary range.
A closer engagement shows seven-inch secondaries causing damage when Ajax cannot keep the range open.
The later assessment blames the close setup, points to the need for destroyer smoke, and notes the impact of fifteen guns.
